# Research {-}

Modelling and data analysis are necessary to support decisions and advocacy efforts. Models are the formalisms that translate data into quantities that answer questions. I develop models to fit to data, to estimate key parameters, and to resolve unknown, implicit, critical assumptions. I develop theory that demonstrates the power of mechanistic mathematical models in data analysis. I am also developing resources to help experts in adjacent disciplines learn about the power of modelling.

## Population dynamics {-}
My research is in [population biology](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/pdf/10.1098/rspb.2019.0428), [mathematical modelling](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16916526/), [evolutionary epidemiology](https://mast.queensu.ca/~tday/pdf/evo12171.pdf), [species range shifts](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12080-022-00551-z), [infectious diseases](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/full/10.1098/rstb.2016.0086) and [public health](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34150314/).

<!-- I am interested in the biological questions. I like to learn new mathematical frameworks, so there are fewer technical barriers to answering the biological questions I am interested in. I have made models that are delay differential equations, partial differential equations, integrodifference equations, non-autonomous ordinary differential equations, branching process models, and models that use optimal control. I aim to not to be limited by the models that I know, in the questions that I can answer. I like to develop new methods, rather than use existing models. -->

<!-- An understanding the general dynamics of models is central to informing what types of models, and interacting biological processes, could give rise to biological data. This approach is useful for answering questions about mechanism, for generating parameter estimates that have a clear connection to biological data and knowledge, and for constructing plausible modelling scenarios. I like to study the full range of model dynamics so I know how to build mechanistic models that will work as representations of biology data that might have unique features. -->




My group's past work has added in physiology and temperature dependence to describe seasonal population dynamics and species ranges shifts in response to climate warming. We have integrated dispersal, movement, and genetic diversity into models to describe spatial dynamics and trait evolution. Our work has been applied to salmon parasites, tiny clams, rabies, drug resistance in hospitals, SARS-CoV-2, bumblebees, wolves, moths, and butterflies.


## Our work during the pandemic {-}

Population biology is a field that has contributed substantially to our knowledge of how [infectious diseases spread](https://scholar.google.com/citations?view_op=view_citation&hl=en&user=1Jd-a_IAAAAJ&citation_for_view=1Jd-a_IAAAAJ:QUX0mv85b1cC). Our modelling work during the COVID-19 pandemic began with a technical report that was included in witness testimony when travel measures implemented under the public health emergency were challenged in provincial court. This work was subsequently peer-reviewed, published, and cited in a World Health Organization report for the Western Pacific Region. We would make many more contributions to the pandemic response including a thorough retrospective analysis of the travel measures implemented in Newfoundland and Labrador during the pandemic [(Mohammadi et al. 2025)](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rsos/article/12/11/241902/356275).

```{r, echo=FALSE, purl=FALSE,out.width="60%", include=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/Principal_Investigator.jpg")
```


Our peer-reviewed and published contributions include:

**Understanding the pandemic in Newfoundland and Labrador**

- [Quantifying SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant spread and the impact of non-pharmaceutical interventions in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013562)

- [Changing contact patterns in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada in response to public health measures during the COVID-19 pandemic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1755436526000083)

- [Extensive SARS-CoV-2 testing reveals BA.1/BA.2 asymptomatic rates and underreporting in school children](https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/reports-publications/canada-communicable-disease-report-ccdr/monthly-issue/2023-49/issue-4-april-2023/ba1-ba2-asymptomatic-rates-school-children.html)

**To support pandemic decision-making in Newfoundland and Labrador**
(and more generally to advance the scientific evidence base to support jurisdictions that implement within-country travel measures and/or an elimination strategy)

- [Importation models for travel-related SARS-CoV-2 cases reported in Newfoundland and Labrador during the COVID-19 pandemic](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rsos/article/12/11/241902/356275)

-  [Is SARS-CoV-2 elimination or mitigation best? Regional and disease characteristics determine the recommended strategy](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rsos.240186)


- [Pandemic modelling for regions implementing an elimination strategy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022519322003691)

- [Downsizing of COVID-19 contact tracing in highly immune populations](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0268586)

- [Rotational worker vaccination provides indirect protection to vulnerable groups in regions with low COVID-19 prevalence](https://www.aimspress.com/article/doi/10.3934/math.2022220)

- [Modelling the impact of travel restrictions on COVID-19 cases in Newfoundland and Labrador](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rsos/article/8/6/202266/96183)

During the COVID-19 pandemic I was quoted in the [British Medical Journal](https://www.bmj.com/content/377/bmj.o994), the [New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2022/04/26/world/canada/canadas-atlantic-provinces-covid-masks.html), the [Globe and Mail](https://www.theglobeandmail.com/canada/article-omicron-making-life-difficult-for-mathematicians-trying-to-track-covid/), and local media such as [NTV](https://vocm.com/2022/02/03/mun-survey-rapid-antigen-tests/), the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/newfoundland-labrador/covid-19-here-to-stay-1.6384033), and [The Telegram](https://www.saltwire.com/atlantic-canada/news/nl-covid-19-statistics-are-going-in-the-right-direction-but-is-there-a-real-goal-100730877/). I appeared in a [Fields Institute Panel](http://www.fields.utoronto.ca/activities/21-22/what-numbers-say-endemicity). I was lead organizer for the [AARMS-EIDM Summer School in Modelling Infectious Diseases](https://ahurford.github.io/aarms-summer-school/).

I am a member of the [Canadian small jurisdictions working group](https://wzmli.github.io/CanSJ/), a group that advances modelling frameworks to support public health in small jurisdictions.

## Publications {-}

For my publications, see [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.ca/citations?hl=en&user=bxt9bh8AAAAJ&view_op=list_works&sortby=pubdate).

When financially possible, our publications are open access. If you can't access a publication, please email me. Below are links to publications that may be more difficult to access. You might also search [MedRxiv](https://www.medrxiv.org/) and [BioRxiv](https://www.biorxiv.org/) for author-typeset copies of the publications.

* [When populations move north but disease moves south: counterintuitive impacts of climate change on disease spread](https://link.springer.com/epdf/10.1007/s12080-022-00551-z?sharing_token=7xNrotHAknrEDzYgQEQPYve4RwlQNchNByi7wbcMAY6GMPsy2wnUpViRSxA82ToFnGewZWnln8z6WcH52FW1BvUD8yoATWUosFpds9E076a1NOTiz0G9s9D_aMHJStojrTBxvfz7s7r-XhThtZ_43WLG6dZct8svMgDt4HuOrhM%3D). See also [Institutional repository ](https://research.library.mun.ca/16406/)

<!-- We had worked on Arctic rabies prior to the pandemic, but just recently I was reminded of the [atto-fox](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1806118/). These problems related to subsequent waves of infection caused by a fractional number of individuals, were central to our [recent work](https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.02.01.24302169v2) explaining common misconceptions that bias against elimination strategy recommendations, and is another example of advances in population biology informing infectious disease modelling. -->
